summary: M.S. Data Science student. I work on how machine learning models for Earth observation get tested, and whether they hold up once the region or the data changes. Right now that is benchmark validity, geographic transfer, and uncertainty under shift.
research_interests: I work on evaluation and out-of-distribution generalization for models that read satellite and Earth data. A lot of what looks like one architecture beating another comes down to how the inputs were set up, things like the channel count, the forecast horizon, or which regions the data came from. So I check whether those differences survive once you seal the holdouts and test on regions the model never saw. More broadly I care about robust computer vision, evaluation under distribution shift, and uncertainty estimates you can actually trust.

in_preparation.title: Train Anywhere, Test Everywhere: Cross-Region Transfer in Earth Observation Is Decided by the Data, Not the Model
in_preparation.note: Sole author, aimed at SIGSPATIAL 2026, a follow-on to the channel-count work. I trained one model per region and tested every model on every region across twenty regions. The training source barely matters. The test region sets the score, the home-field edge is -0.001 FoM, and the regions rank the same way across six architectures, fine-tuned foundation models included. A one-line rule that just extends each region's recent past beats every trained model (FoM 0.56 against 0.34, IoU 0.92 against 0.61). Change the input instead and transfer falls along a spectrum, with harmonized products carrying over and raw reflectance hardly carrying at all. The same pattern shows up again on the PANGAEA benchmark. Code at github.com/rohanbalixz/Cross-Region-Source-Invariance-in-Earth-Observation.

in_preparation.title: Confident but Unidentifiable: Positivity Limits on Cross-Region Attribution in Geospatial Models
in_preparation.note: Sole author. Submitted to Transactions in GIS (Wiley), under review. It reframes the question of why a geospatial model fails in a new region as a transportability problem and asks when a covariate explanation is even identifiable. I derive a positivity diagnostic and a partial-identification bound computable from the source data alone, with a threshold past which not even the sign of an attribution is recoverable. On land-cover transfer from the contiguous US to 44 cities across four world regions, overlap with the training data drops as low as 5.8%, and a confident, well-fit explanation predicts the wrong sign of the transfer gap in every architecture that degrades. Code at github.com/rohanbalixz/Confident-but-Unidentifiable-Positivity-Limits-on-Cross-Region-Attribution-in-Geospatial-Models.

research_projects.title: Confident but Unidentifiable: Positivity Limits on Cross-Region Attribution
research_projects.description: Asks whether covariate-based explanations of why a geospatial model fails in a new region are identifiable at all, framed as a transportability problem with a source-computable positivity diagnostic and partial-identification bound. Submitted to Transactions in GIS (Wiley), under review.
highlights: A source-computable threshold past which not even the sign of an attribution is recoverable
Land-cover transfer from the contiguous US to 44 cities across four world regions, with covariate overlap as low as 5.8%
A confident, well-fit explanation predicts the wrong sign of the transfer gap in every degrading architecture

research_projects.title: Multi-Horizon Urban-Growth Prediction (Channel-Count Confound)
research_projects.description: Continental-scale benchmark audit of CNN and ConvLSTM comparisons on multi-horizon urban-growth prediction using GHSL R2023A over CONUS.
highlights: Sealed 2020 temporal holdout, with channel-matched horizon controls
MC Dropout calibration r = 0.983 across 8.69M validation pixels
SpatialDI 2026 (Springer LNCS) main paper. GeoAI 2026 short paper (oral), published on Zenodo. SpatialDI PDF forthcoming.

research_projects.title: Learning from Minimal Data under Distribution Shift (Master's thesis)
research_projects.description: How to learn from little labeled data when the distribution is also moving. It sits somewhere between representation learning, robustness, and evaluation.
highlights: Investigating robustness and adaptation in low-data regimes
Empirical study of representation-learning trade-offs under shift
